1. Introduction
---------------

Nanotechnology is a term used to define areas of science and engineering in which phenomena occurring at nanoscale dimensions are used in the design, characterisation, manufacture, and applications of materials, structures, devices, and systems. The concept of nanotechnology was first introduced in 1959 when physicist Richard Feynman presented a presentation on making things at the atomic and molecular levels. Nanotechnology is now regarded as the most promising technology of the twenty-first century, and researchers have investigated it as a novel technique in medical research. Nanotechnology will usher in a new era of productivity and prosperity, as seen by increased public funding for nanotechnology research and development over the last decade.[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0001), [2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0002) Nanotechnology can spur economic growth and enhance capacity and quality in industrial sectors. It has substantially contributed to society's welfare and moulded the nature of modern living. It has the potential to significantly alter societal dynamics, economic conditions, and human life.

Man has been looking for miraculous remedies to relieve sickness and [injury](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/injury "Learn more about injury from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pain for hundreds of years. Many academics feel that nanotechnology applications in medicine can be essential in achieving this. These applications include comprehensive surveillance, control, creation, repair, and defence of all biological human systems, employing designed [nanodevices](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanodevice "Learn more about nanodevices from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [nanostructures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanostructures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") functioning from a molecular level. Nanotechnology can transform medical research radically and create a new sector of human improvement.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0003), [4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0004), [5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0005) There is a good line for nanotechnology's diagnostic, therapeutic, and preventative applications. This technology can intentionally modify the body, among many problems. The nano drugs employed have demonstrated that bioavailability is enhanced, [side effects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/side-effect "Learn more about side effects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are eliminated, and therapeutic medicine is absorbed more effectively.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0006)-[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0007)

The presence and entry of every single molecule in the brain essentially structure a highly selective membrane barrier permeable for high partition coefficient molecules. [Nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nanoparticle "Learn more about Nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have recently been used on this membrane as a medication carrier system. Particularly, nanoparticles are inhaled and cross brain membranes. The typical and traditional therapies for vascular thrombosis often have relatively limited advantages due to the short plasma half-life, many [adverse effects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/adverse-event "Learn more about adverse effects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and fast drug wash-outs. To improve the stability and even half-life of the encapsulated medication, immobilising a specific quantity of an agent in a [drug delivery system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/drug-delivery-system "Learn more about drug delivery system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") might overcome this restriction. [Biocompatibility](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/biocompatibility "Learn more about Biocompatibility from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and biodegradability make [polymeric nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-nanoparticles "Learn more about polymeric nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and liposomal [nanocarriers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nanocarrier "Learn more about nanocarriers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are often employed.[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0008), [9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0009), [10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0010)

Science and technology have been used for illness diagnosis, treatment and prevention of trauma, pain relief, and the conservation and enhancement of human health via molecular instruments and molecular understanding of the human body. Most current commercial uses in medicine in nanotechnologies aim at medication supply. New modes of action may be introduced, and existing medicinal compounds are better targeted and more bioavailable. The [nanoprobes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanoprobe "Learn more about nanoprobes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), integrated sensory nanoelectronic systems, and multifunctional [chemical structures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/structure-composition "Learn more about chemical structures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for pharmaceuticals delivery and disease targeting are upcoming applications in nanotechnology.[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0011), [12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0012), [13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0013)

There has already been a great success in enhancing medication delivery with nanotechnology. Specific compounds may improve the aiming and entrance of medication into the cell and improve the imaging, intracellular targeting, and controlled release of therapeutic genes. Therefore, clinicians could detect and improve their influence on sick cells and tumours to optimise therapy dosages. In combination with other forms of personalised treatment, nanometric can be customised to focus on an individual patient is only sick cells, minimising adverse effects and [tissue damage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/tissue-injury "Learn more about tissue damage from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Scientists have already made a few advances that promote cell development to treat [spinal cord damage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/spinal-cord-lesion "Learn more about spinal cord damage from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). [Magnetic nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/magnetic-nanoparticle "Learn more about Magnetic nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and nano parts sensitive to enzymes that target brain tumours; intelligent nanoparticles samples for delivery of intracellular drug products and gene expression imaging; quantitative points to detect and quantify human brain cancer.[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0014), [15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0015), [16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0016)

In healthcare area, nanotechnology opens new limits in the life sciences industry. Nanotechnology has great promise in manipulating things at the atomic level to change many parts of medical treatment, such as diagnosis, monitoring for diseases, operating equipment, regenerative medicine, developing vaccines, and medication delivery. It also opens the way through sophisticated research instruments to develop drugs to improve treatments for various ailments. Nanotechnology can be utilised for medication to particular cells in the body, thereby reducing the risks of failure and rejection.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0017), [18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0018), [19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0019) We have identified four primary research objectives of this paper as under: (1) to identify types of Nanotechnology and [Nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanoparticle "Learn more about Nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with their uses in the medical field; (2) to discuss classes and taxonomy of Nanotechnology-based-materials for the medical sphere; (3) to identify & discuss associated features and characteristics of Nanotechnology for the medical domain; and (4) to identify contemporary and future Nanotechnology applications for the medical field.

2. Need for nanotechnology in the medical field
-----------------------------------------------

Nanotechnology and nano [drugs discoveries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/drug-discovery "Learn more about drugs discoveries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are so vast and wide-ranging. [Nanomedicine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanomedicine "Learn more about Nanomedicine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has undergone impressive modifications, pushing the drug to a new level with significant healthcare outcomes. There is a need to study the significant capabilities of nanotechnology in healthcare. In medicine, extensive research is ongoing into best practices and methodologies, including nephrology, cardiovascular disease therapeutic gene, and cancer therapy. There is a significant development in the traditional treatment, and the quality of [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and nanotechnology have improved and shown encouraging results.[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0020)-[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0021) Gene therapy has also taken advantage of nano drugs. Several investigations focused on the applications of viral vectors believed to be systems for medication delivery.[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0022), [23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0023), [24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0024)

Nanobots targeting specific cancer cells to smart tablets send data back to researchers to ensure patients receive their treatment correctly. Nanotechnology offers the potential for in-vitro diagnosis by substituting existing procedures for more economical alternatives that are easier to utilise. Nanoparticles can operate as molecular imaging agents within those devices and input cancer-related genetic alterations and tumour cell functional features. Further, functional nanotechnology-based coatings frequently include the following [nanomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), depending on the desired function: titanium dioxide, [silicon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/silicon-dioxide "Learn more about silicon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), carbon black, [iron oxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/iron-oxide "Learn more about iron oxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [zinc oxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/zinc-oxide "Learn more about zinc oxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and silver. Instruments and procedures enhance physio-chemical characterisation assessments, safety, and effectiveness of [nanomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanocrystalline-material "Learn more about nanomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and nano surfaces integrated into [medical device](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/medical-device "Learn more about medical device from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") engineering. Scientists play a crucial part in creating goods that include [novel materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/novel-material "Learn more about novel materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), sensors, and energy storage systems.[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0025)-[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0026)

3. Different types of nanoparticles are being used in the medical field
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Nanoparticles have a significant surface-area-to-volume ratio due to their nanoscale size, which allows them to absorb vast amounts of medications and move quickly throughout the bloodstream. Their increased surface area gives them distinct capabilities, as it increases their mechanical, magnetic, optical, and catalytic qualities, allowing them to be used in more pharmaceutical applications. Nanoparticles are classified into three areas based on their chemical composition: organic, inorganic, and carbon-based.[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0027), [28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0028), [29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0029)[Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#fig0001) shows some primary classifications of the nanoparticles. Proteins, carbohydrates, lipids, and other organic molecules are synthesised into organic nanoparticles with a specific dimension, such as a radius of fewer than 100 nm.

Inorganic nanoparticles are non-toxic, hydrophilic, biocompatible, and highly stable compared to organic materials. Inorganic nanoparticles include elemental metals, [metal oxides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/metal-oxide "Learn more about metal oxides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and metal salts, among other things. [Fullerenes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/fullerene "Learn more about Fullerenes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [carbon nanotubes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/carbon-nanotube "Learn more about carbon nanotubes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), graphene, and its derivatives are examples of carbon-based nanomaterials. These materials have piqued the attention of various fields, including biomedical applications, because of their unusual structural dimensions and exceptional mechanical, electrical, thermal, optical, and chemical properties. Nanoparticles generally retain the chemical properties of their bulk materials, which can be beneficial when selecting a nanoparticle for various applications.[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0030), [31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0031), [32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0032), [33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0033)

The nanoparticles respond to the light by getting hot enough to destroy cancer cells. Researchers believe that nanoparticles can directly be put into circulation and become cancer tumours in the Future. Smart pills are medication releases with ingestible sensors that can be managed wirelessly and adjusted to control a medicine dose depending on data collected throughout the body. [Nanomedicine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanomedicine "Learn more about Nanomedicine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") faces inherent obstacles like every innovative and revolutionary technology; mainly, it has been widely used in clinical applications. The environmental impact of nanotechnology accumulates in live tissues and organs and can be made affordable on a big scale. Nanobots are a significant advance in nanomedicine.[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0034), [35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0035), [36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0036), [37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0037)

4. Classes and taxonomy of nanotechnology-based-materials for medical sphere
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

[Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#fig0002) explores the various classes and categorisations of different nanotechnology-based materials having special applications and utilities in the domain of medicine. Several classes in the available literature have been reported, namely; [metal nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/metal-nanoparticle "Learn more about metal nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [dendrimers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/dendrimer "Learn more about dendrimers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [liposomes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/liposome "Learn more about liposomes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [biodegradable polymers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/biodegradable-polymer "Learn more about biodegradable polymers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), carbon-based nanomaterials, hydrogel nanocomposites, and many others too that support and expends the utilisation and services in the medical domain through the concept of nanotechnology.[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0038)-[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2414644723000337#bib0039)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Nanotechnology enhances targeted drug delivery, improves diagnostic accuracy, and accelerates vaccine development, exemplified by the rapid creation of COVID-19 mRNA vaccines.
Nanotechnology optimizes energy systems through advanced materials like nanostructured solar cells and improved battery technologies, leading to enhanced efficiency and reduced energy consumption.
Nanotechnology contributes to environmental protection via innovative materials for pollution reduction, water purification, and improved waste management systems, promoting a cleaner ecosystem.
Nanotechnology enhances consumer products, improving durability and safety, while also enabling smarter packaging that can monitor freshness and detect contaminants.
